sections.header: Verses 1-8 — The Siege Brick and the Prophet's Body

heb.paragraph: Ezekiel engraves Jerusalem on a mud brick (levenah) and builds siege works against it. Babylonian clay tablets were the standard writing medium — the exiles would have recognized this as a model or map. The prophet turns familiar Babylonian media into a prophetic weapon against their own city.

Ezekiel bears the iniquity (avon) of Israel and Judah by lying on his side — 390 days for Israel, 40 for Judah. The word avon carries a triple meaning: sin, guilt, and its punishment. Ezekiel symbolically absorbs all three through his body.

panels.ctx: This is the first of Ezekiel's dramatic sign-acts (Hebrew: ot, 'sign'). Unlike other prophets who primarily spoke, Ezekiel acts out his prophecies with his own body. The siege model is street theater — performed before the exilic community to dramatize Jerusalem's coming fate. The 390 + 40 days echo Israel's wilderness wandering (40 years) and the long history of northern apostasy.

cross.note: Isaiah walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign against Egypt — prophetic sign-acts have precedent.
Forty years of wandering for forty days of spying — the day-for-a-year principle Ezekiel applies.
Bearing one another's burdens — Ezekiel literally bears Israel's sin, a foreshadowing of vicarious burden-bearing.

notes.note: The brick with Jerusalem inscribed and siege works built around it is a miniature model of what Nebuchadnezzar will do to the actual city. The iron pan as a wall between prophet and city symbolizes God's separation from Jerusalem.
The 390 days for Israel may represent the years from Solomon's apostasy to the fall of Jerusalem, while the 40 for Judah recall the wilderness punishment period.
God binds Ezekiel so he cannot turn — the sign must be carried out completely. Divine judgment, once pronounced, will not be turned aside.
Calvin sees the siege model as God accommodating his message to human weakness — the exiles could not grasp abstract prophecy, so God gave them a visual demonstration.
Calvin interprets the lying on each side as priestly intercession: Ezekiel bears the sin of the people as a mediator, foreshadowing the ultimate burden-bearer.
The Hebrew levenah refers to a sun-dried mud brick typical of Mesopotamian construction, not a fired brick. Maps and city plans on such bricks have been found in archaeological excavations.
The LXX reads 190 days instead of 390 — the textual variant is significant and may represent different calculations of Israel's sinful period.

Block argues that the sign-acts are not merely illustrative but performative — they enact the coming reality in the prophetic realm. The siege brick does not just picture Jerusalem's fall; it participates in bringing it about through prophetic authority.

Zimmerli identifies three originally independent sign-acts that have been combined: the siege model (vv.1-3), the lying on the side (vv.4-6), and the binding (vv.7-8). Each makes a distinct theological point about Jerusalem's coming destruction.

sections.header: Verses 9-17 — Siege Rations and Defiled Bread

heb.paragraph: The command to cook bread over human dung strikes at Ezekiel's priestly identity — a priest trained in purity laws is commanded to defile himself. When Ezekiel protests (one of only two times he objects in the book), God relents to cow dung. Even the compromise is shocking: exile will force defilement.

panels.ctx: The mixed-grain bread and rationed water symbolize siege famine — the mixing of grains violates the principle of separation (kilayim), and cooking over dung violates priestly purity (Lev 7:19-21). For the priestly prophet, this is a profound theological crisis: exile means living in a state of permanent ritual impurity among the nations.

cross.note: Food that touches anything unclean must not be eaten — the purity law that exile will make impossible.
Daniel's refusal to eat defiled food in Babylon contrasts with Ezekiel's forced acceptance of defilement — two responses to the crisis of exile purity.

notes.note: The mixed grains represent famine conditions — when food is scarce, people combine whatever is available. This violates the normal dietary practices of a prosperous nation.
Ezekiel's only recorded protest in the book: 'I have never defiled myself.' God's concession to cow dung shows mercy even within judgment.
Breaking the staff of bread — a metaphor for cutting off food supply — will reduce Jerusalem to despair and wasting.
Calvin admires Ezekiel's appeal: the prophet does not refuse the mission but appeals to God's own law. God honors this principled objection by adjusting the method while maintaining the message.
The six ingredients (wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, spelt) represent siege rations — whatever grain remains is mixed together, violating the purity of separation.
Twenty shekels of food per day (about 8 ounces) is starvation rations — roughly one-third of minimum caloric needs.

Block highlights the priestly dimension: for Ezekiel the priest, defilement is not just physical but theological. Exile is not merely geographical displacement but liturgical death — separation from the source of purity. The bread sign-act makes this abstract horror concrete.

Zimmerli sees Ezekiel's protest as a key moment of characterization: unlike other prophets, Ezekiel does not argue about his mission but about ritual purity. His priestly formation shapes even his objections to God.

lit.note: Three interlocking sign-acts portray Jerusalem's siege: the model city, the prophet's immobilized body, and famine rations.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: the entire chapter dramatizes Jerusalem's coming destruction. Holiness at 8: the defilement of the bread strikes at the heart of priestly purity.

Babylonian clay tablets were the standard writing medium — the exiles would have recognized this as a model or map. Ezekiel bears the iniquity (avon) of Israel and Judah by lying on his side — 390 days for Israel, 40 for Judah. The word avon carries a triple meaning: sin, guilt, and its punishment. Ezekiel symbolically absorbs all three through his body.

The command to cook bread over human dung strikes at Ezekiel's priestly identity — a priest trained in purity laws is commanded to defile himself. When Ezekiel protests (one of only two times he objects in the book), God relents to cow dung. Even the compromise is shocking: exile will force defilement.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: This is the first of Ezekiel's dramatic sign-acts (Hebrew: ot, 'sign')…

ppl.name: Nebuchadnezzar
ppl.text: The brick with Jerusalem inscribed and siege works built around it is a miniature model of what Nebuchadnezzar will do to the actual city…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Siege of Jerusalem

translations.text: Then lay siege to it: Erect siege works against it, build a ramp up to it, set up camps against it and put battering rams around it.
And put siegeworks against it, and build a siege wall against it, and cast up a mound against it. Set camps also against it, and plant battering rams against it all around.
Turn your face toward the siege of Jerusalem and with bared arm prophesy against her.
And you shall set your face toward the siege of Jerusalem, with your arm bared, and you shall prophesy against the city.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "The brick with Jerusalem inscribed and siege works built around it is a miniature model of what Nebuchadnezzar will do to the actual city. The iron pan as a wall between prophet and city symbolizes Go…"
